prompt: What are the 2026 emerging STANDARDS for AI AGENT IDENTITY, authorization, and provenance that a security-attestation product must support to be SOTA: NIST AI agent / agentic AI guidance, SPIFFE/SPIRE for workload identity, OAuth 2.1 + token-bound agent credentials, the IETF/OpenID work on agent auth, MCP (Model Context Protocol) security, Anthropic/OpenAI agent identity schemes, W3C verifiable credentials for agents, and any 'agent passport' / 'agent attestation' specs. For each: what it specifies, adoption status, and what an attestation report should capture about an agent's identity + scopes + delegation. Cite primary sources (URLs, @handle+date). Conclude with exactly what kolm should add to its report to be the agent-identity SOTA.
content:
**NIST AI Agent / Agentic AI Guidance (primarily via the AI Agent Standards Initiative and related RMF profiles/concept papers):** NIST’s February 2026 launch of the AI Agent Standards Initiative (via CAISI) focuses on voluntary, industry-led standards for secure, interoperable agentic AI. It emphasizes identity/authentication, authorization, security controls, risk management (aligned with the NIST AI RMF’s Govern/Map/Measure/Manage functions), interoperability (e.g., via protocols like MCP), and research into agent security. A February 2026 NCCoE concept paper specifically addresses accelerating adoption of identity and authorization standards/best practices for software/AI agents, including questions on current/emerging standards, technologies, identification/authorization/auditing/non-repudiation, and mitigations (e.g., for prompt injection). Related work includes an Agentic AI Risk-Management Standards Profile (Berkeley CLTC, Feb 2026) mapping to NIST AI RMF.[[1]](https://www.nist.gov/artificial-intelligence/ai-agent-standards-initiative)[[2]](https://www.nist.gov/news-events/news/2026/02/announcing-ai-agent-standards-initiative-interoperable-and-secure)[[3]](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/other/2026/02/05/accelerating-the-adoption-of-software-and-ai-agent/ipd)

Adoption status (as of June 2026): Initiative active since Feb 2026 launch, with RFI on security considerations (comments due ~March 2026), concept paper comment period (closed April 2026), listening sessions, and industry feedback emphasizing flexible/voluntary, risk-based, interoperable approaches. No finalized mandatory standards yet; focuses on convening stakeholders and influencing bodies like ISO/IEC. Complementary profiles (e.g., Berkeley) provide practical mappings.[[4]](https://www.cybersecuritydive.com/news/ai-agents-security-nist-industry-feedback/814434/)

Attestation report should capture: Agent’s alignment with RMF functions (e.g., governance policies, risk mappings, measurement controls); identity/auth mechanisms used (e.g., standards supported); authorization scopes and delegation chains (with auditability/non-repudiation); security controls/mitigations applied (e.g., against specific risks); compliance attestations tied to the initiative or profiles.

Primary sources: NIST AI Agent Standards Initiative page (nist.gov, announced Feb 17, 2026); NCCoE concept paper (csrc.nist.gov, published Feb 5, 2026, comments due April 2, 2026); Berkeley CLTC Agentic AI Profile (Feb 2026 PDF).[[1]](https://www.nist.gov/artificial-intelligence/ai-agent-standards-initiative)[[3]](https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/other/2026/02/05/accelerating-the-adoption-of-software-and-ai-agent/ipd)

**SPIFFE/SPIRE for workload identity:** SPIFFE defines a standard for cryptographically verifiable workload identities via SPIFFE IDs (URIs like `spiffe://trust-domain/path`) and SVIDs (short-lived JWT or X.509 certificates). SPIRE is the runtime implementation handling attestation (proving workload provenance without secrets, via selectors like Kubernetes labels, process info, or runtime env). It supports non-human identities (NHIs) and is explicitly discussed for AI agents as flexible for dynamic, provenance-bound identities (distinct from static service accounts).[[5]](https://www.solo.io/blog/agent-identity-and-access-management---can-spiffe-work)[[6]](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/blog/spiffe-securing-the-identity-of-agentic-ai-and-non-human-actors)

Adoption status: Mature for traditional workloads/service meshes (e.g., Istio, Kubernetes); actively extended/recommended for AI agents in 2025–2026 literature and implementations (e.g., JWT SVID auth to secrets managers, relationship-based auth). Not yet universal for agents but positioned as a foundational layer alongside OAuth/etc.

Attestation report should capture: Agent’s SPIFFE ID and SVID details (type, issuer, expiry, claims); attestation evidence/selectors (runtime provenance, env bindings); policy-enforced scopes; delegation/relationship metadata if extended.

Primary sources: spiffe.io (spec); various 2025–2026 analyses (e.g., Solo.io June 2025, HashiCorp April 2026).[[5]](https://www.solo.io/blog/agent-identity-and-access-management---can-spiffe-work)[[6]](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/blog/spiffe-securing-the-identity-of-agentic-ai-and-non-human-actors)

**OAuth 2.1 + token-bound agent credentials:** OAuth 2.1 (IETF draft) consolidates security improvements: mandatory PKCE, removal of implicit grant, sender-constrained tokens (DPoP, mTLS), refresh token rotation/binding, and stronger client auth. Extensions for agents include token exchange (RFC 8693), on-behalf-of flows, transaction tokens, identity assertion grants, and CIBA. Emphasizes binding credentials to agents for scoped, auditable, non-replayable access.[[7]](https://aembit.io/blog/mcp-oauth-2-1-pkce-and-the-future-of-ai-authorization/)[[8]](https://github.com/aaronpk/oauth-v2-1/blob/main/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1.md)

Adoption status: Advancing IETF draft (v2.1 iterations in 2024–2026); widely referenced/integrated (e.g., MCP requires it); OpenID Foundation and agent-specific drafts build on it. Production use growing for agents via IdPs.

Attestation report should capture: OAuth client/agent ID and auth method; issued tokens (claims like sub, aud, scopes, act_as/delegation chains, binding mechanisms like DPoP proofs); token provenance/expiry; resource indicators or audience restrictions.

Primary sources: IETF draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1 (datatracker.ietf.org); OpenID whitepapers/drafts (openid.net, e.g., 2025–2026).

**IETF/OpenID work on agent auth:** Includes IETF drafts (e.g., draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00 proposing OAuth/OIDC-based model for agent interactions, identity assertions, chaining, discovery); OpenID Foundation AI Identity Management (AIIM) CG whitepaper “Identity Management for Agentic AI” (Oct 2025) covering challenges, OAuth 2.1/MCP integration, delegation, and gaps; related efforts like OIDC-A extensions, WIMSE (workload identity), and transaction tokens.[[9]](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00)[[10]](https://openid.net/new-whitepaper-tackles-ai-agent-identity-challenges/)

Adoption status: Active I-Ds (e.g., March 2026 draft); OpenID CG work since ~Oct 2025 with whitepaper and NIST RFI response (March 2026); influencing broader standards.

Attestation report should capture: Agent identifier/credentials (cryptographic bindings); delegation/authorization chains (with validation); attestation/verification mechanisms; supported flows (e.g., assertions, exchange); cross-domain or workload federation details.

Primary sources: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00 (datatracker.ietf.org, March 2, 2026); OpenID “Identity Management for Agentic AI” whitepaper (openid.net, Oct 7, 2025); OIDF NIST response (March 2026).[[9]](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth-00)[[10]](https://openid.net/new-whitepaper-tackles-ai-agent-identity-challenges/)

**MCP (Model Context Protocol) security:** MCP enables LLM/agent integration with tools/data sources. Security/auth spec (updated 2025) mandates/recommends OAuth 2.1 + PKCE for authorization flows; uses Protected Resource Metadata (PRM/RFC 9728), resource indicators (RFC 8707) to scope tokens and prevent misuse; supports dynamic client registration or Client ID Metadata Documents (CIMD); emphasizes user consent, audience restriction, and treating MCP servers as OAuth resource servers.[[11]](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/tutorials/security/authorization)[[12]](https://auth0.com/blog/mcp-specs-update-all-about-auth/)

Adoption status: Spec revisions through 2025 (e.g., March/June 2025 updates); growing adoption in agent/tool ecosystems (Anthropic, etc.); referenced in NIST initiative.

Attestation report should capture: MCP client/server roles and auth flows; OAuth tokens/metadata (scopes for tools/resources, resource indicators, consent records); security considerations addressed (e.g., token binding, replay prevention).

Primary sources: modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/ (authorization section, 2025 revisions, e.g., 2025-03-26/2025-06-18).

**Anthropic/OpenAI agent identity schemes:** Anthropic emphasizes secure boundaries (e.g., vaulted OAuth tokens separate from sandboxes, MCP proxies for tool calls, narrow scoping, no static secrets in agent env). OpenAI’s Agents SDK and related frameworks (plus community efforts like ZeroID) focus on scoped credentials, tool calling with auth, delegation/handoffs, and verifiable identities (often OAuth/SPIFFE-backed). Both prioritize runtime, just-in-time, cryptographically bound credentials over static/shared secrets.[[13]](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/managed-agents)

Adoption status: Product features and SDKs in 2025–2026 (e.g., Anthropic MCP/tool support, OpenAI agent primitives); influencing broader ecosystems via MCP and partnerships.

Attestation report should capture: Agent-specific credential mechanisms (e.g., proxied/vaulted tokens, SDK primitives); scopes/delegations; boundary/separation attestations; provenance or binding details.

Primary sources: Anthropic engineering blogs (e.g., anthropic.com/engineering, 2025–2026 posts); OpenAI community/developer resources and SDK docs.

**W3C verifiable credentials for agents (incl. DIDs):** W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model (v2.0, 2025) defines tamper-proof, cryptographically verifiable claims (e.g., identity, capabilities, authorizations) issuable by organizations. Combined with DIDs for self-sovereign/agent-controlled identifiers; enables delegation chains, proofs, and revocation. Emerging W3C Community Group on Agent Identity Registry (2026) specifies DID methods and VC formats tailored for agents binding to orgs.[[14]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.02841)[[15]](https://www.w3.org/community/agent-identity/)

Adoption status: Core VC DM mature/standardized; agent-specific applications and DID methods in research/prototypes (2025 arXiv papers) and new W3C CG (April 2026); early adoption in decentralized/multi-agent scenarios.

Attestation report should capture: Agent DID; issued/presented VCs (claims on identity, scopes, capabilities, delegations/issuers); cryptographic proofs/signatures; verification status/revocation; cross-domain trust elements.

Primary sources: W3C VC DM v2.0 (w3.org/TR/vc-data-model-2.0, May 2025); W3C Agent Identity Registry CG (w3.org/community/agent-identity, April 2026); related arXiv papers (e.g., 2511.02841, Dec 2025).

**Agent passport / agent attestation specs:** Emerging specs include Workday’s Agent Passport (launched ~June 2026) for pre-deployment testing/verification + continuous monitoring against standards (OWASP LLM Top 10, NIST AI RMF, MITRE ATLAS), with signed, auditable attestations by trusted parties (e.g., Cisco). Open Agent Passport (OAP) is an open spec for pre-action authorization: cryptographically signed credentials binding agent identity to authorized capability scopes, with policy evaluation, audit records, and interception of tool calls.[[16]](https://investor.workday.com/news-and-events/press-releases/news-details/2026/Workday-Launches-Agent-Passport-to-Test-Verify-and-Continuously-Monitor-Every-AI-Agent-in-the-Enterprise/default.aspx)[[17]](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.20953v1)

Adoption status: Workday early access H2 2026 (GA by end-2026); open specs/proposals active (2026 GitHub/arXiv); tied to broader attestation trends.

Attestation report should capture: Signed test/verification results vs. named standards; capability/scope bindings; delegation/provenance; continuous monitoring status; cryptographic signatures/attestors; policy outcomes.

Primary sources: Workday press release (investor.workday.com / prnewswire, ~June 2026); OAP arXiv paper (e.g., 2603.20953, March 2026).

**What kolm should add to its report to be the agent-identity SOTA:** Support capturing and including (with cryptographic verification where possible): (1) SPIFFE/SPIRE SVIDs + attestation selectors/provenance; (2) OAuth 2.1 token claims/bindings (scopes, delegation chains via act_as/exchange, DPoP/mTLS proofs); (3) MCP-specific auth metadata (resource indicators, PRM, consent); (4) IETF/OpenID agent auth elements (assertions, chaining); (5) W3C VC/DID presentations with claims on identity/scopes/delegations; (6) NIST RMF/profile compliance mappings and controls; (7) Agent passport-style signed attestations (tests vs. OWASP/NIST/MITRE, continuous monitoring results); (8) explicit delegation graphs/chains, runtime provenance, and revocation status across all. Structure the report to map these to standards with verifiable artifacts for interoperability and auditability. This positions kolm as the comprehensive attestation layer bridging workload (SPIFFE), web (OAuth/MCP), decentralized (VC/DID), and emerging agent-specific (NIST/passport) standards.
